text:
Admission Date:  2014-04-07       Discharge Date:  1974-04-06

Date of Birth:   2014-04-18       Sex:  M

Service:  CARDIOTHORACIC SURGERY

HISTORY OF THE PRESENT ILLNESS:  The patient is a 63-year-old
patient of Dr. Williamson referred for outpatient cardiac
catheterization.  The patient had an inferior MI in 1979.
Catheterization at that time revealed 100% POB, 90% apical
LAD.  The patient was doing well and most recently had angina
while walking to the train station accompanied by mild
shortness of breath.  The pain was exacerbated by walking.
The patient denied pain at rest.  ETT in 2014-10-07 was
significant in that the patient exercised 6 minutes, 20
seconds.  Positive EKG change, [**Street Address(2) 2051**] depression in leads
II, III, aVF, and V4 and V6.  Large reversible lateral
defect, apical reversible defect, and inferior fixed defect,
EF 47%.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1.  Hypertension.
2.  GERD.
3.  H. pylori.
4.  MI in 1979.

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
1.  Cholecystectomy in 1992.
2.  Abdominal hernia repair in 2006.

ALLERGIES:  The patient has no known drug allergies.

ADMISSION MEDICATIONS:
1.  Aspirin 81 mg q.d.
2.  Zestril 10 mg q.d.
3.  Allopurinol 100 mg q.d.
4.  Nexium 40 mg p.r.n. as needed for heartburn.

HOSPITAL COURSE:  The patient underwent a CABG times five
with LIMA to the proximal LAD, SVG to the diagonal to the
distal LAD, SVG to the OM and SVG to the PDA.  The patient
tolerated the procedure without complications and continued
to do well.  The patient was extubated on postoperative day
number one and was transferred to the floor on postoperative
day number two.

The patient continued to have an uncomplicated course and was
felt to be ready for discharge on postoperative day number
five.  The patient was cleared by Physical Therapy,
ambulating well, tolerating a regular diet, and had good p.o.
pain control.  The patient will be going home with a
follow-up with Dr. Williamson in four weeks and with his primary
care doctor, Dr. Williamson, in one to two weeks, and his
cardiologist in two to three weeks.

MEDICATIONS ON DISCHARGE:
1.  Lopressor 50 mg b.i.d.
2.  Amiodarone 400 mg b.i.d. times one week and then q.d.
until follow-up with Cardiology.
3.  Protonix 40 mg q.d.
4.  Allopurinol 300 mg q.d.
5.  Percocet one to two tablets q. four to six hours p.r.n.
6.  Ibuprofen 400 mg q. six hours p.r.n.
7.  Tylenol 650 mg q. four hours p.r.n.
8.  Aspirin 325 mg q.d.
9.  Colace 100 mg b.i.d.
10. Lasix 20 mg b.i.d. times seven days.
11. Potassium chloride 20 mEq b.i.d. times seven days.
12. Milk of magnesia 30 milliliters q.h.s. p.r.n.

CONDITION ON DISCHARGE:  Good.

DISCHARGE STATUS:  To home.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:  Status post coronary artery bypass
graft times five.



                      DR.Williamson,Frank 02-248
